title: Free Speech vs Government Visa Bans
source: The Guardian - The Economist  WEB  06 -12  June 2026
content:
The recent decision by the British government to block controversial American influencers from entering the country has sparked a heated debate about free speech. Cenk Uygur and Hasan Piker were both denied entry to speak at prominent events, raising questions about whether liberal democracies are abandoning their core values. This standard of excluding individuals whose presence is deemed not conducive to the public good is extraordinarily vague and increasingly utilized. Such decisions erode the cultural norm that people should be able to say and think what they want. While direct incitement to violence is unlawful, simply holding offensive or disturbing views should not be grounds for a visa ban.

Banning foreign speakers does not prevent citizens from hearing their views, as anyone can easily access their social media accounts online. In fact, attempting to suppress their speech often backfires by generating more interest and driving up their online search metrics. Provocateurs who thrive on internet engagement are undoubtedly delighted by the extra attention generated by these government bans. Parliament should press the government to stop using its powers so casually and instead focus on protecting actual physical safety. If a nation makes a habit of banning visiting speakers, it risks appearing censorious and ultimately makes its society less free.
